critique.summary: The article reports on Oregon Governor Tina Kotek's visit to Estacada High School to assess the impact of a statewide executive order banning student cell phone use during the school day. The policy has been positively received at Estacada, with teachers and students noting improved classroom engagement and reduced distractions, though some challenges remain.
mainPoints: Governor Tina Kotek issued an executive order banning cell phone use by students during the school day in Oregon's K-12 public schools.
Estacada High School and its district have embraced the ban, reporting improved classroom discourse and teacher satisfaction.
Students noted positive changes in social interactions and reduced distractions but mentioned difficulties with scheduling and access to some educational resources.
The policy was implemented statewide with all public school districts in Oregon in compliance.
Estacada School District has seen significant academic improvements in recent years, with a 92.5% graduation rate in 2025.
The district allows students to keep phones in backpacks for emergency contact and parental tracking, rather than using lockers or pouches.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about the executive order banning cell phones in Oregon public schools and the positive feedback from Estacada High School are consistent with publicly available information about Governor Tina Kotek's policy initiatives. The graduation rate figures for Estacada High School align with reported improvements in some Oregon districts, although independent verification of the exact graduation rates and the direct impact of the phone ban on academic outcomes would require additional sources. The article largely relies on statements from local officials and the governor, presenting a generally accurate but somewhat one-sided view without extensive external data or critical perspectives.
considerations: The article does not provide data or studies quantifying the impact of the phone ban statewide or beyond Estacada.
It lacks perspectives from students or educators who may oppose or have concerns about the ban.
Potential negative effects on students who rely on phones for accessibility or educational purposes are briefly mentioned but not deeply explored.
The article does not discuss how enforcement challenges or variations in implementation across districts might affect outcomes.
There is no mention of any independent research or expert opinions evaluating the policy's effectiveness.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the recent Oregon school cell phone ban, with commenters generally supporting the ban for reducing distractions and improving focus, while some highlight enforcement challenges and concerns about top-down mandates. There is also debate about the role of technology in education and the balance between safety, control, and modern communication needs.

comments.author: tejohnso
comments.text: Not having distraction devices in a classroom is such a basic concept. I'm surprised it required government intervention. Every half decent school principal should've banned them in their school, and if the principal didn't, the individual teachers should have banned them from their classrooms. The first time a kid had to have a question repeated to them because they were looking at their phone should've been the last time phones were permitted in that class.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights the common sense nature of banning phones to reduce distractions, reflecting a widely accepted educational perspective.

comments.author: mattbaker
comments.text:
It’s the right idea but it also puts the burden of enforcement on teachers that are already over extended, especially in schools where behavioral challenges are more prevalent. Great in a scenario where students are compliant, and a nightmare in environments where they’re not.

I don’t have a solution to that problem, but I also think it’s important to acknowledge it’s not all sunshine and roses.

I’m saying this as a person with close friends in Oregon school systems, based on the experiences they’ve shared with me.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully addresses the practical difficulties of enforcing phone bans, especially in challenging school environments, which is a valid concern.

comments.author: dgxyz
comments.text:
UK here. My kid's school is insane. They think they are so progressive because they banned personal phones entirely, which is fair enough. But they forced us to buy marked up Yondr pouches, which is not fair.

However this isn't the only problem. They also force us to pay monthly for iPads with wonky ass Logitech cases to be issued on which they do everything on Google classroom.

Google Classroom is an abhorrently bad bit of software on an iPad. It's just horrible in every possible way. Clunky, interface sucks, slow, unreliable.

Then they give detentions when children can't submit work, some auth issue means the entire device goes down the toilet for two days, documents won't open because the staff use Office instead, they keyboard case craps out and you can't type with anything but the screen, the staff forget to submit the work until an hour before it's due, the entire school wifi network is down for a week and they have no backup.

They should ban that too. Technology MUST be fit for purpose in a classroom and most of it isn't.

Go back to paper for everything. Work, journals, timetables, the lot. And the teachers can use whatever to drive projectors in the classroom.
comments.analysis: Provides a detailed, credible account of technology-related problems in schools, emphasizing that banning phones alone is insufficient without good alternatives.

comments.author: wjholden
comments.text:
For what it's worth — my last workplace did not allow cell phones in the building and I learned to love it. When people attended meetings, we all made eye contact and talked about the task at hand. Nobody ever got distracted by notifications or tuned out with boredom. And since we all had traditional telephones at our desks, someone would come get you if your family was calling with for an urgent crisis. I miss it.

My kids' school banned phones during the school day. The principal promised that the office would relay any messages if parents call, and they do. I would be interested to see if there are already statistics showing academic success. That is, are grades and test scores affected by phone bans? The article talks about graduation rates, but doesn't directly address grades and scores.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a personal positive experience with phone bans and raises a valid point about the need for empirical data on academic outcomes.

comments.author: technothrasher
comments.text: My son, who recently graduated high school, went to a school that banned phones but insisted on laptops (providing them for the kids who couldn't afford one).  He said it was ridiculous, as none of the kids had any problem using their laptop for anything they would have used the phone, which was mostly texting, scrolling social media, watching videos, and playing games.  Even when the school tried to lock down services, as soon as one kid found a way around it, they all did.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights the challenge of substituting one device for another without addressing underlying distraction issues, a reasonable observation.

comments.author: dizzy9
comments.text:
> In crafting its policy, Estacada incorporated feedback from parents. That led to some key decisions around the cell phone ban. Rather than use pouches or lockers, students are allowed to keep their phones safely stored in their backpacks. That was for two reasons — it allows students to contact loved ones during emergencies, and many parents use phone trackers to keep tabs on their kids.

I'm glad to hear this. They're currently trying to shill the magnetically sealed pouches in the UK, but the flaws are obvious: massive bottleneck at the pouch station would delay entry and exit from the building, phones would be unavailable during emergencies or to record incidents of crime or staff malpractice, and financial burden on schools.

Students can be trusted to obey a simple "no phones in class" rule.
comments.analysis: This comment thoughtfully considers practical and safety concerns, supporting a balanced approach to phone bans.

comments.author: jmward01
comments.text: I think this is likely a good concept for schools, but I want to see the data and not opinions. Lack of evidence based policy is what got us here, we should at some point start using evidence based policy to get us out of it.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly emphasizes the importance of evidence-based policymaking rather than relying solely on anecdotal opinions.

comments.author: alexfoo
comments.text:
I think this more about it coming from a higher authority than the school itself.

Many schools have similar bans but they don’t get support from many of the pupils or their parents as both groups have members that just believe it is the school choosing to overstep their authority.

Now it is a diktat from above it makes the school’s job in enforcing it much easier. They can just point to the relevant legislation/diktat and say that their hands are tied, if you disagree here are the places you can go to voice your opinion. Meanwhile we (as a school) have no choice but to apply the rules, etc.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully explains how top-down policies can facilitate enforcement by reducing local resistance.

comments.author: nabbed
comments.text:
I would love it if my laptop had a "study mode" for when I am trying to debug something or learn something new using my laptop. Some of us have less than stellar self-control, so a study mode which requires a multi-step rigamarole to shut off might prevent me from casually checking my email or a news website when I am supposed to be learning a new data structure or figuring out a data corruption bug. I have no idea how it would work in real life: I need access to the internet to lookup API documentation, download libraries, and read online books, but I imagine something could be worked out.

(This article mentions that not only are cell phones banned at the featured school, but these kids have hobbled laptops that supposedly help them focus on school work, although the imperfect nature of the hobbling has unintended consequences).
comments.analysis: This comment proposes a nuanced technological solution to distraction, showing thoughtful engagement with the problem.

comments.author: bananamogul
comments.text:
"decreed by executive order"

That's the only bummer here. I do agree with this policy, but no one voted for it. The governor just said "you're going to do this".

Yes, yes, I know - people elected the governor. But this sort of policy seems like something that should require legislative approval, not just one person deciding the whole state must do something.

For every time something good comes of that kind of behavior, there's 10 times when it's a disaster.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid governance concern about the process of implementing such policies, highlighting democratic procedure issues.

comments.author: mystraline
comments.text:
For a country that likes to brag about being a "democratic republic", the 2 major areas of society (school and work) are the most fascistic top-down authoritarian structures we have.

And sure we can vote every 2 years. Yay.

But what freedom do we have when schools can steal student's property, or a business owner can fire you for speech made outside of work.
comments.analysis: While somewhat emotional, this comment reflects a common critique of institutional authority but lacks specific evidence.

comments.author: superkuh
comments.text: It's crazy to me that cell phones, and especially smart phones, were ever allowed in the classroom during class.
comments.analysis: Expresses a widely held opinion about phones being a distraction, though stated in a blunt manner without elaboration.

comments.author: ronbenton
comments.text: I remember reading somewhere else that there was a psychological benefit for kids as well. Not having the constant pressure to check the device. Just seems like a big win all around.
comments.analysis: Highlights an important mental health aspect of the ban, aligning with research on device-related stress.

comments.text: Now ... what do we do about the rest of society. This problem isn't just a school problem, it is whole of society, escpecially senior citizens. They are more prone to the problems of phones, social media, and continuous disinformation ... and they vote.
comments.analysis: Broadens the discussion to societal impacts of phone and social media use, a relevant but less focused comment.

comments.author: caderosche
comments.text:
I don't think banning is the right solution to this. At some point, I think we are going to have comms devices imbedded in our heads and whatnot.

I think the right approach is finding teaching techniques that still work when every human has all the world's info at their finger tips 24/7.

At some point, an uninterruptible, 24/7 live connection to the rest of the world is inevitable.

I'm not convinced a human teacher is a required part of this.
comments.analysis: Offers a forward-looking perspective on education adapting to technology rather than banning it, a valid viewpoint.

comments.author: briffle
comments.text: its a blanket rule, which has almost no exceptions.  So there are some silly parts.  One of my kids is in band and the school uses YONDER pouches.  They have had to dig out some really, really old analog tuners to use.  They have a fraction of the capability of a $4 IOS app, but the kids are supposed to keep their phones in a special sleeve with no exceptions... (so many kids break that rule, or throw an old dummy phone in the pouch)
comments.analysis: Points out practical inconsistencies in enforcement that can undermine the policy's effectiveness.

comments.author: bawolff
comments.text: I'm a little confussd... was there a point they were allowed? I went to school in the late 2000s, and even at that point if a teacher saw you with a cell phone it was immediately confiscated.
comments.analysis: Provides historical context that phone restrictions have long existed, suggesting the current ban is not novel.

comments.author: ecshafer
comments.text: I agree with the cell phone bans (I would extend it to all electronic devices, schools should be pen and paper). But we just got our phones taken away in highschool.
comments.analysis: Supports the ban and suggests even broader restrictions, reflecting a strict stance on technology in schools.

comments.author: mmaunder
comments.text: Given the free market nature of cellphones, where vendors and companies have unfettered access to monetize users, having cellphones in school is akin to making school children line up and listen to sales pitches from companies around the world for several hours a day, instead of focusing on education.
comments.analysis: Uses a metaphor to emphasize commercial exploitation via phones, a valid concern though somewhat hyperbolic.

comments.author: reedf1
comments.text: My understanding is that these are already banned in most schools and the practical difference between enforcing this at a state or national basis is basically nonexistent vs simple local enforcement.
comments.analysis: Accurately notes that the policy may formalize existing practices rather than introduce new changes.

comments.author: SunshineTheCat
comments.text:
It's tough to imagine how different it must be for kids now than when I went to school.

I know there's a billion other reasons, but I've heard parents say they want their kid to have a phone so they can keep in touch if they need to.

When I was a kid, cell phones weren't a thing (at least for kids) so the once or twice a year I needed to call a parent I went down to the office and asked to use their phone.

Then I got to have whatever, usually embarrassing, conversation with my mom while everyone in the school office stared at me. Good times.
comments.analysis: Provides nostalgic context and explains parental concerns, contributing to understanding the issue.

comments.author: kleiba
comments.text: This has absolutely been the standard in every school around where I live for years. Anecdotally, however, I wouldn't go so far and say it lead to "engaged students" and "joyful teachers" :)
comments.analysis: Offers a skeptical view on the claimed benefits, highlighting that bans alone may not improve engagement or morale.

comments.author: selectively
comments.text: Phone bans are bad.
comments.analysis: This comment is overly brief and lacks supporting arguments, making it unhelpful to the discussion.
